source_alignment.subject: English
source_alignment.source_statement: Discuss and evaluate how authors use language, including figurative language, considering the impact on the reader.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack turns broad discussion of authorial choice into an evidence routine: notice a precise choice, interpret it in context, describe a supportable reader effect and compare a purposeful alternative without pretending that every reader responds identically.

objective.child_goal: I can explain how a writer's precise choices shape meaning, mood and the reader's view.
objective.statement: Identify and evaluate purposeful vocabulary, imagery, sentence and viewpoint choices, supporting claims about meaning and reader effect with precise textual evidence.
misconceptions: Names a technique or interesting word without explaining its meaning in this context.
Claims that a choice makes the reader want to read on or feel excited without precise evidence.
Treats a possible reader response as certain and identical for every reader.
Assumes longer, rarer or more figurative language is automatically more effective.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A deliberate choice a writer makes about words, images, sentences or viewpoint.
vocabulary.audio_script: An authorial choice is something the writer selects to shape meaning or effect.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A writer's control panel labelled word, image, sentence and viewpoint.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Ideas or feelings suggested by a word as well as its basic meaning.
vocabulary.audio_script: Connotations are the extra ideas and feelings a word can carry.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: One word linked to a small web of suggested ideas.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A response the text may encourage, supported by evidence rather than guessed.
vocabulary.audio_script: A reader effect is a response the writer's choice may encourage.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The position or perspective from which events and ideas are presented.
vocabulary.audio_script: Viewpoint controls what the reader sees, knows and notices.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Two observation windows showing different details of the same event.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Compare 'The door opened' with 'The door crept open'. What changes when the writer chooses crept?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Activate precise word comparison before naming techniques.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Notice the exact changed word, then say what it suggests in this sentence.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Two sentence strips differ in one highlighted word.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Build a chain: exact choice, meaning in context, possible reader response.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Establish an evidence-led model that separates textual observation from interpretation.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Start with what is actually written. Explain what it suggests here, then use may, might or encourages for the reader response.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Three linked panels labelled CHOICE, MEANING and POSSIBLE EFFECT.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: The storm 'clawed at the station walls'. Trace how clawed shapes the scene.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model contextual connotation, personification and bounded effect language.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Clawed suggests repeated animal-like attack. This makes the storm seem hostile and may make the station feel vulnerable.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The word clawed connects to repeated attack, hostile storm and vulnerable station.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Replace one choice, reread both versions and decide which better fits the writer's purpose.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Evaluate effect through controlled comparison rather than technique spotting.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Keep the event the same. Change one word, sentence pattern or viewpoint choice, then compare what each version foregrounds.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A split editor aligns original and alternative with an evidence note beneath each.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Which explanation is stronger: 'It makes you read on' or one that names what the words suggest and why that matters here?
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A strong explanation can point back to exact language and the situation in the text.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: An evidence beam reaches the precise explanation but not the generic claim.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach your companion which choice matters most, what it suggests and one reasonable limit on your claim.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Gather independent evaluation, evidence selection and epistemic caution.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Quote briefly, explain the contextual meaning and say the response the choice may encourage.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A quote, interpretation and possible-effect card remain visible during explanation.

manipulatives.purpose: Select the smallest useful word or phrase and connect it to contextual meaning.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners highlight a precise span, choose or compose a contextual connotation and reject nearby evidence that does not support the claim.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Keyboard span controls, line focus, adjustable spacing, read-aloud by sentence, non-colour selection states and a phrase-list alternative avoid precision-drag and visual-memory barriers.

manipulatives.purpose: Build and revise a choice-meaning-possible effect explanation with calibrated language.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners connect one quotation to an interpretation and a possible response, then test whether each link remains justified in context.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Cards can be selected rather than dragged; every connector is also a numbered text statement; sentence frames, oral response and one-link-at-a-time mode are available.

manipulatives.purpose: Compare original and alternative vocabulary, sentence or viewpoint choices while holding core content constant.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners toggle one controlled change, compare versions and justify which better serves a stated purpose; more than one answer may be accepted when evidence is coherent.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Differences are announced and underlined as well as highlighted; versions can be heard separately; no speed score is used; and the unchanged sentence frame remains visible.

practice.interleaving_notes: Begin with one-word contrasts in short prose and poetry; then interleave imagery, sentence rhythm, sound pattern and viewpoint across narrative and non-fiction, revisiting inference and vocabulary while keeping quotation load proportionate.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Select a precise word or short phrase and explain what it suggests in its immediate context.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: 60-120 word passage + target claim + selectable spans + contextual connotation choices + brief explanation
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid vocabulary obscurity as the main challenge; reviewers must verify that the selected span genuinely supports the intended interpretation.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Explain how imagery, comparison, repetition or sound pattern contributes to meaning, mood or emphasis.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: short prose/poetry extract + exact pattern + meaning cards + possible effect + evidence chain
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Do not require terminology when a clear explanation is stronger; effects must be text-specific and expressed as possibilities.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Compare sentence length, ordering and punctuation choices in relation to pace, focus and clarity.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: matched event + two sentence-pattern versions + stated purpose + comparison + quoted justification
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid universal rules such as short sentences always create tension; context and surrounding rhythm must make the comparison defensible.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Explain how viewpoint and information selection shape sympathy, uncertainty and what the reader notices.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: paired viewpoint extracts + known/hidden detail map + evidence selection + bounded response claim
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use age-appropriate social situations without coercive emotional content; distinguish narrator knowledge from character knowledge.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain authorial-choice reasoning through concise substitutions and evidence checks across spaced review.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: one sentence + stated purpose + 3-4 plausible substitutions + meaning/effect comparison + confidence check
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Alternatives must be grammatical and meaningfully contrastive; reward fitness for purpose rather than sophistication.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Names a technique or interesting word without explaining its meaning in this context.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The response labels metaphor, verb or short sentence but could be copied unchanged onto a different extract.
repair_sequence: Replace the technique label with what the choice suggests here.
Connect that contextual meaning to the scene, idea or character.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner gives a context-specific interpretation of a fresh quotation without relying on a label alone.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Claims that a choice makes the reader want to read on or feel excited without precise evidence.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The reader-effect statement is generic or has no traceable link to the selected language.
repair_sequence: Explain its connotation, emphasis or information effect.
Complete the frame: this may encourage the reader to notice, expect or feel because.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: A new effect claim includes a valid evidence-to-meaning chain.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Treats a possible reader response as certain and identical for every reader.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The learner uses proves, makes everyone or definitely feel for an interpretive response.
repair_sequence: Use may, might, can or encourages.
Name the evidence that makes the response reasonable while allowing alternatives.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner offers a bounded response and recognises another plausible evidence-based interpretation.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Assumes longer, rarer or more figurative language is automatically more effective.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The learner chooses the most complex alternative without reference to purpose, clarity, voice or context.
repair_sequence: Compare one controlled change while keeping content constant.
Choose the version whose meaning, rhythm or viewpoint best serves that purpose.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner justifies a simpler or more complex choice according to purpose rather than prestige.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use a calm Orbit City reading console with static text, one evidence link at a time and no ambient animation behind passages.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace evidence beams, rhythm pulses and viewpoint masks with underlines, numbered links and before-and-after still panels.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Provide human-reviewed passage narration with sentence and paragraph replay; preserve the printed quotation and never use automatic prosody as evidence of authorial effect.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Offer line focus, adjustable spacing, glossary-in-context, short extract mode and a phrase list that mirrors text highlighting.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Keep the routine visible as CHOICE-MEANING-POSSIBLE EFFECT-CHECK and chunk longer extracts before comparison.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Remove timers, allow repeated reading, keep original and alternative versions visible and accept oral, typed or structured evidence chains.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Credit accurate noticing before interpretation, label responses as evidence-based possibilities and allow revision without losing a valid quotation.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach key literal meanings and cultural references, distinguish dictionary meaning from contextual suggestion and accept clear evidence-based explanations in accessible language.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires precise evidence and a context-specific meaning/effect explanation across phrase highlighting, comparison and explanation. Secure requires evaluation of sentence or viewpoint choices, calibrated reader-response language and a justified alternative interpretation across two retained sessions.
evidence.parent_summary: Can identify a writer's precise choices and explain, with evidence, how they shape meaning, mood, pace or viewpoint.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Stores selected spans, contextual interpretations, effect links, comparison decisions and claim calibration separately so reading comprehension is distinguishable from terminology, transcription or motor demand.
next_actions: Route technique-only answers to contextual connotation.
Route generic effects to the choice-meaning-effect chain.
Route certainty claims to fact-versus-interpretation comparison.
Schedule mixed vocabulary, imagery, sentence and viewpoint retrieval at 1, 3, 7, 14 and 30 days.

qa.notes: Review-stage Year 5 reading bank now reaches the 240-item pilot target with four preserved curated questions and deterministic candidates across every blueprint and format. Generated candidates require human curriculum, teacher, accessibility and safeguarding review for extract quality, interpretive plausibility, cultural assumptions, narration and alternative evidence-based responses before promotion.

accessibility_policy.no_timer_or_penalty: Learners are never timed for mastery and never lose earned progress for mistakes; incorrect attempts route to calm repair, replay and retry.
accessibility_policy.equivalent_response_routes: Every child-facing task must preserve an equivalent route through touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing, partner-mediated or adult-scribed response where appropriate.
accessibility_policy.audio_equivalent: Audio and read-aloud support must complement visible text, visuals and replay. Child-facing narration must use produced, human-listening-approved assets before release; browser TTS is not a teaching or phonics fallback.
accessibility_policy.reduced_motion: Reduced-motion and static alternatives must preserve the same curriculum evidence, feedback and reward meaning without relying on animation.
